Overview
Manual medicine, or the use of hands used for healing, goes back 
to ancient times. In the early days of medicine, practitioners had 
only their hands to use in the art of healing. In addition, the 
therapeutic touch advantage of laying on of hands is immeasur -
able. The therapeutic benefit includes tremendous emotional sup-
port to the patient and a transfer of energy from the practitioner 
to the patient. There are various methods of applying manual 
medicine, and there are some overlaps of techniques with 
 chiropractic and osteopathic medicine. Many other clinicians, 
including physical therapists, massage therapists, and other prac -
titioners, utilize manual techniques for patient care, both as pri -
mary methods of healing and as adjuncts to care. Patients with 
myofascial pain, low back pain, and cervicogenic pain can experi-
ence significant relief from manual medicine techniques. With the 
high percent of adults who will experience either low back pain 
(approximately 80%) or neck pain (approximately 50%) in their 
lifetimes, it is not surprising that the use of manual techniques is 
often incorporated in the care of these patients.
69,94,96,252  Although 
overall estimates of the total expenditures on low back care vary, 
the consensus is that more than $90 billion/year is spent on the 
diagnosis and management, and an additional $10 to $20 billion 
is attributed to economic losses in productivity each year.
96 Many 
physical medicine and rehabilitation (PM&R) residency pro -
grams incorporate some form of manual medicine teaching into 
the training, and the American Academy of Physical Medicine 
and Rehabilitation (AAPM&R) has regularly incorporated 
 educational sessions with hands-on training in the use of manual 
techniques. This chapter reviews the principles behind manual 
medicine along with other modalities, including traction and 
massage, as they are incorporated into the practice of PM&R.
Historical Perspective
Manual medicine dates back to the time of Hippocrates  
(460–377 BCE) and Galen (131–202 BCE). 164,168 However, 
more modern clinicians during the sixteenth and seventeenth 
centuries utilized manual medicine techniques, which deviated 
from the traditional disease-oriented form of medicine of the 
times. However, manual medicine lost its popularity until the 
nineteenth century, when other clinicians emerged in England 
known as “bonesetters.”
51,168 During the nineteenth century, two 
pioneers in manual medicine were generating enthusiasm at about 
the same time. These included Andrew Taylor Still, who is be -
lieved to be the founder of osteopathic medicine in 1874, and 
Daniel  D avid Palmer, who similarly was the founder of chiroprac -
tic medicine in 1895.51,115 It was Still who utilized the concept of 
wellness and wholeness in his osteopathic principles that largely 
focused on the body’s self-healing mechanism. He felt strongly 
that structure and function were interrelated and that a person 
needed normal functioning of both to have homeostasis. Manual 
medicine techniques were utilized to achieve this goal of normal 
function and normal structure. It is these founding principles that 
• Spinal manipula tion/mobilization is effective in adults for acute, 
subacute, and chronic low back pain; migraine and cervico -
genic headache; and cervicogenic dizziness.
• T here is moderate-quality evidence that manipulation interven -
tions may produce a small to moderate reduction in pain 
 intensity compared with other forms of treatment.
• P atients should be educated about potential side effects asso -
ciated with manual medicine even when treatment is success -
ful. These include increased autonomic effects (hypotension, 
perspiration, etc.) and a transient potential increase in 
 discomfort.
17
Manipulation, Traction, and Massage
RONALD B. TOLCHIN, JAMES E. EUBANKS, AND JAMES W. ATCHISON
• M ore than 20 million Americans receive some form of manual 
medicine each year.
• Struc ture and function are interrelated and both are needed to 
have homeostasis, and manual medicine techniques are utilized 
to achieve normal function and structure.
• T hrust techniques or mobilization with impulse is an activating 
force applied by the practitioner through the restrictive barrier 
using the principles of joint play.
• Str ain-counterstrain is a technique that utilizes hypershortening 
of the muscle thereby working on the intrafusal fibers and 
 reducing the firing of the gamma system.
• A utonomic activation likely plays an important role in the clini -
cal effects associated with osteopathic manipulative treatment.
SUMMARY PEARLS

still guide graduates of osteopathic medical schools today. Clini -
cians trained in osteopathic medical schools not only get the 
conventional core curriculum of modern medicine but also the 
incorporation of these principles and the art of manual medicine 
utilized to treat abnormalities of structure and function used in 
the art of healing. Chiropractic medicine utilizes the relationship 
between the spine and normal health as it relates to the nervous 
system. It is these mechanical impairments of the spine that are 
believed to impact health, and the correction of the dysfunction 
within the spine (subluxation) can bring about improvement in 
health.
There has also been interest through traditional medicine in 
the use of joint manipulation, particularly through British clini -
cians John Mennell and James Cyriax. Cyriax, a British orthope -
dic surgeon, has had many publications involving manipulation, 
massage, traction, and injections.93 One pioneer in the American 
sector, Janet T ravell, utilized the manual medicine techniques in 
her approach to myofascial pain. Along with David Simons, they 
published numerous journals and two popular textbooks utilizing 
the concepts of trigger points and spray and stretch. 325 Further, 
there is a group known as the Fédération Internationale de 
 Médecine Manuelle that represents manual medicine practitio -
ners throughout the world.
Definitions
The International Federation of Manual Medicine defines ma -
nipulation as “the use of hands in the patient management process 
using instructions and maneuvers to maintain maximal, painless 
movement of the musculoskeletal system and postural balance.”316 
The goal of manual medicine is to restore homeostasis to the body 
and improve motion in areas of restriction, which will promote 
posture and optimize function in a pain-free range. 105,115,269 In 
addition, it may help to restore altered reflex patterns by improv -
ing range of motion (ROM) and body symmetry, correct muscle 
imbalances, and improve overall tissue texture by reducing areas 
of tightness. It is important to start with an overall structural 
evaluation prior to treatment and then a reevaluation upon com -
pletion of treatment.105,218,270 There are various forms of manual 
medicine, and common approaches include manipulation of spi -
nal facet joints, manipulation of peripheral joints, and myofascial 
techniques. It is important to focus on areas of restriction and 
promote ease of motion, restoring optimal muscle function over -
all. After this, the patient will begin to have a decrease in pain and 
an improvement in functional mobility that can have an impact 
on efficiency of gait with less energy cost and fatigue. There may 
also be improvement in physiology, including decreasing nocicep-
tion, decreasing gamma gain, enhancing lymphatic return, im -
proving circulation, and restoring homeostasis to the body, which 
may be achieved by reducing afferent nociceptive impulses into 
the spinal cord and promoting the natural endorphin release from 
the body.157,184,224
The popularity of manual medicine continues to rise among 
chiropractic and osteopathic physicians and therapists with exper-
tise in manual medicine skills. More than 20 million Americans 
receive some form of manual medicine each year. 279,290 Each 
 student who enters osteopathic medical school (Doctor of 
 Osteopathic Medicine [DO]) has osteopathic manipulation in -
corporated throughout the four years of education. Allopathic 
physicians (Medical Doctor [MD]) may also learn manual medi -
cine techniques through continuing educational courses or 
 programs affiliated with osteopathic medical schools, at annual 
AAPM&R meetings, or even online courses. A Doctor of Chiro -
practic (DC) receives manual medicine skills as the primary 
means of education throughout the four years. Physical therapists 
(PT) and massage therapists have manual medicine techniques 
incorporated into their educational curriculum and have the abil-
ity to take additional focused courses on the various forms of 
manual medicine. It is important to understand that with rising 
medical costs, manual medicine techniques may offer a cost- 
effective treatment option for many patients. However, the phys-
iatrist needs to be aware of the indications and contraindications 
to all forms of conservative treatment options, especially in the 
case of manual medicine techniques.
The effectiveness and risks of manual medicine can some -
times be challenging to study and demonstrate. It is difficult to 
accurately measure tactile or manual approaches to treatment 
and the effect of therapeutic touch. One must account for the 
potential effects of touch as it pertains to the placebo effect on 
the human body. Many studies have shown the benefits of 
manual medicine techniques compared with conservative con -
ventional care. However, there is the continued need for large, 
randomized controlled studies to determine long-term benefits. 
Issues with many of the studies include variability in techniques 
utilized, intertester reliability, participant and treater blinding, 
validated outcome measures, and the challenges with sham tech -
niques that may utilize therapeutic touch and may in fact have 
their own placebo effect.
Manual Medicine Types
Barrier Concept
Before moving forward with the various forms of manual medi -
cine, it is important to understand a core concept that resonates 
throughout all forms of manual medicine. This includes the 
concept of joint asymmetry with restriction of motion. Each 
joint in the body has a normal range of motion unique to that 
joint. If there is a restriction of motion within that normal 
range, then there is a motion barrier that restricts that joint from 
achieving its full ROM. This then becomes the restrictive bar -
rier, or the area that is restricted from achieving normal joint 
motion. This is simply measured as a functional restriction and 
is defined as “somatic dysfunction” that occurs within the nor -
mal ROM of the joint. This can then lead to asymmetry of that 
area of the body. Manual medicine is then utilized to restore 
normal motion back to that area of restriction. This does not 
apply to dislocation, which would involve movement beyond 
the anatomic barrier and would include significant tissue dam -
age. These concepts are more easily understood through the 
schematic in Fig. 17.1.
Classification of Manual Medicine Techniques
The various manual medicine techniques will be described in 
great detail further into the chapter; however, they can be under -
stood through a breakdown of the techniques, including soft tis -
sue–based techniques, articulatory techniques, or specific joint 
mobilization.218 In turn, the techniques can be broken down into 
direct and indirect techniques. Direct techniques move the body 
part or joint in the direction of the restrictive barrier, whereas 
indirect techniques involve moving the body part or joint away 
from the restrictive barrier into the position of ease. There are also 
combined methods that may be utilized as well as physiologic 
response methods, such as respiratory assistance or exaggerated

movements, for both direct and indirect techniques. The latter are 
activities performed by the patient to increase the effect of the 
concomitant practitioner’s techniques. Further, one can utilize 
extrinsic forces that are applied from the outside of the body as 
well as gravitational forces. These can utilize straps or pads on the 
table in addition to the practitioner who may be utilizing a 
springing technique, guiding technique, or thrusting technique. 
Intrinsic forces occur within the person’s body and include muscle 
forces, respiratory forces, and inherent forces such as fluctuations 
in body fluid pressure.
105
A skilled practitioner must learn to palpate muscle, tendon, 
and fascia, which may show signs of vasodilation, edema, flac -
cidity, contracture, or various combinations of these. These may 
in turn lead to changes in skin temperature or texture changes, 
moisture, bogginess, ropiness, thickening, or muscle firm -
ness.
39,105,162,269 The practitioner must be skilled at assessing 
these tissue changes and the response to treatment. The exam -
iner should also look for symmetry or asymmetry of joint 
 motion both statically and during gait. It is important to under -
stand that asymmetry may direct the practitioner to the area of 
somatic dysfunction. ROM should be assessed in all body parts 
and can be noted both grossly and segmentally to palpation and 
to observation. The practitioner should also be able to deter -
mine multilevel vs. segmental abnormalities and areas of hypo -
mobility or hypermobility. The osteopathic practitioner may 
utilize a set of 10 screening tests as part of a comprehensive ex -
amination, popularized by Phillip Greenman ( Table 17.1).
105 
There are five theoretical models to describe the types of  treatments 
utilizing manual techniques. These include (1) postural-struc-
tural, (2) neurologic, (3) respiratory-circulatory, (4) bioenergy, 
and (5) psychosocial.

• Fig. 17.1 A model of somatic dysfunction. The first three panels depict 
production of somatic dysfunction from a mechanical cause. The last 
three panels depict treatment positions with thrust technique, isometric 
muscle energy technique, and indirect balancing. (A) A normal joint is 
positioned in the center, with free motion available in either direction. The 
shaded area at either end depicts the end of permitted motion. The out -
side line is termed the anatomic barrier. If joint motion goes beyond this 
point, structural damage will occur. The Glossary of Osteopathic Terminol-
ogy describes the anatomic barrier as the endpoint of passive motion and 
the physiologic barrier as the endpoint of active motion. (B) This panel, 
injury, shows a force moving the center of the joint to the right. (C) The 
third panel, somatic dysfunction, shows the effect of the injury. The neutral 
point is now positioned to the right. The muscle on the right is contracted 
and shortened; the muscle on the left is stretched (strained). Motion to the 
right is freer; motion to the left exhibits bind or restriction. This is asym -
metric motion, which is typical of spinal somatic dysfunction. There is re -
striction of motion to the left so that the range of motion is impaired. The 
endpoint of this restriction of motion to the left is termed the restrictive 
barrier. Think of the barrier as a series of restrainers that are preventing 
motion to the left, as contrasted to a brick wall preventing motion. The 
short, tight muscle on the right prevents full motion to the left. The last 
three panels show treatment position. (D) Thrust technique (impulse, high 
velocity, and low amplitude) is a direct technique. The barrier is engaged 
by moving the joint to the left. The final corrective force is a practitioner 
force. (E) Direct isometric muscle energy technique looks very similar on 
the panel. The barrier is engaged by moving the joint to the left. The phy -
sician holds and asks the patient to contract against the holding force. 
The shortened, contracted muscle is the one that is contracting. (F) Indi -
rect balancing involves moving the joint to the right, away from the restric-
tive barrier. The tension should be balanced on both sides. Final corrective 
force is a release by inherent forces.

Direct Techniques
Thrust technique or mobilization with impulse is an activating force 
applied by the practitioner through the restrictive barrier using the 
principles of joint play, defined by Mennell.
256 This involves a high-
velocity, low-amplitude (HVLA) type of thrusting maneuver. These 
techniques are more commonly associated with the term manipula-
tion. Articulatory techniques utilize a low velocity and higher ampli-
tude. They may also involve an oscillation at the restrictive barrier.
Direct myofascial release (MFR) techniques are a type of load-
ing technique involving stretching of the tissues, holding the tis-
sues at the position of restriction, and then waiting for a release of 
tension and improvement in ease of motion.
Indirect Techniques
Strain-counterstrain (SCS) is a technique that utilizes hypershortening 
of the muscle, thereby working on the intrafusal fibers and reducing 
the firing of the gamma system. This is usually done for 90 seconds 
while the practitioner monitors the painful area and finds a point of 
ease and reduction of pain and then carries the restricted joint area 
back to a normal resting state with an improvement in ROM. Indirect 
balancing, functional, balanced ligamentous tension, indirect MFR, 
and craniosacral are all examples of indirect techniques. Craniosacral 
technique utilizes the body’s inherent fluid forces by mobilizing the 
brain, meninges, and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).
Spinal Mechanics
An understanding of spinal mechanics and principles of motion 
are important foundations in manual medicine, which are 
 attributed to Harrison Fryette.140 When the spine is either under-
going flexion or extension, there is no coupled movement in the 
sagittal plane from the facets; however, when rotation or side 
bending is introduced, the facets are aligned in such a way that, 
although rotation and side bending occur together, they occur to 
opposite sides. For example, if one side bends to the right, then 
that group of vertebrae that are side bent to the right will naturally 
rotate slightly to the left. This is called coupled motion and occurs 
in a relatively neutral spinal position where there is no substantial 
flexion or extension. This is type I or neutral spinal mechanics, 
according to Fryette ( Video 17.1). However, when an individual 
goes into extremes of flexion or extension, the mechanics will 
change. T o incorporate the coupled motion of side bending op -
posite to that of rotation, one segment will rotate the same way as 
the side bending component. This can be illustrated when some-
one goes to pick something off the floor by bending at the waist 
and rotating and side bending, for example, toward the right. The 
hyperflexion that is needed to achieve that motion will engage one 
segment at the apex of the curve that will rotate and side bend to 
the right, whereas all other vertebrae that are side bent to the right 
will rotate to the left. This is type II mechanics or nonneutral 
mechanics, according to Fryette ( Video  17.2). These principles 
apply to most of the spine; however, they do not apply to  
the cervical spine, in which the conventional cervical vertebrae 
from C3 through C7 side bend and rotate to the same side. The 
exceptions to this are C1, which is mostly involved in flexion or 
extension, and C2, which is mostly involved in rotation. These 
segments are the so-called atypical segments of the cervical spine 
(Video 17.3). In addition, the sacrum does not behave in this way

Somatic Dysfunction
As noted previously, manual medicine techniques are utilized to 
treat motion restriction. The term used to describe motion restric-
tion is manipulatable lesion. Somatic dysfunction is defined as im-
paired or altered function of related components of the somatic 
(body framework) system: skeletal, arthrodial, and myofascial 
structures, and related vascular, lymphatic, and neural elements.
105 
This is listed in the 2024 International Classification of Diseases  
(diagnostic codes M99.0–M99.09). 77,275 Somatic dysfunction is 
defined through palpation and includes alterations to tissue tex -
ture, increased sensitivity to touch or hyperalgesia, altered ROM, 
and anatomic asymmetry or positional change.
118,119,138,139,223,224,269 
Another way to remember somatic dysfunction is through the 
mnemonic: Tenderness combined with structural Asymmetry and 
altered Range of motion and Tissue texture changes (TART). Joint 
play is usually decreased, and there is muscle spasm or tissue tex -
ture changes associated with the restricted joint. Early findings of 
this can be manifested as vasodilation, edema, pain, and tender -
ness, all of which are often present acutely. In the chronic state, 
however, there is fibrosis, paresthesia, itching, and persistent ten -
derness often present. A dysfunction should be named in three 
planes of motion and used to compare the superiorly placed seg -
ment to the inferior segment. This is particularly true with regard 
to the spine. For purposes of definition, somatic dysfunction de -
scribes the position of ease. For example, a type I dysfunction from 
L2 to L5 could be neutral, side bent right, and rotated left. This 
would describe that these segments as a group are easily moved 
into side bending right and rotational left in a neutral plane. A 
type II dysfunction, or nonneutral dysfunction, could describe L3 
nonneutral flexed, rotated, and side bent to the right, which de -
scribes the motion of ease for a single segment (see earlier).
The Educational Council on Osteopathic Principles utilizes a 
single point on the most anterior superior part of the vertebral 
body to describe its position. For extension, this part would move 
backward, and for flexion it would move forward. For right rota -
tion, the point would move to the right as when looking at it from 
behind; for right side bending, this point would side bend to the 
right. For ease of understanding, one could visualize the transverse 
processes moving to the right or left. If the transverse processes are 
more prominent on the right, that would imply that these verte -
brae are rotated to the right. What the practitioner would palpate 
would be a relative fullness of the paraspinal muscles overlying  
the transverse process caused by the rotation of the transverse 
 processes.
Physiologic Rationale for Manual Therapies
Gamma System
T wo types of motor neurons, alpha and gamma, exit the spinal 
cord through the ventral rami to innervate skeletal muscle. Both 
of these neurons have cell bodies located in the ventral horn of the 
spinal cord and are modulated through the reticulospinal tract. 
Alpha motor neurons innervate large skeletal muscle fibers 
through extrafusal fibers. At the functional level, a motor unit 
consists of a single alpha motor neuron and the skeletal muscle 
fibers it innervates. Gamma motor neurons innervate intrafusal 
fibers within muscle bellies and receive information about muscle 
length or rate of change of muscle length from muscle spindles. 
Muscle spindles contain a sensory component that transmits 
mechanoreceptive and proprioceptive information, in addition to 
a motor component comprised of the gamma motor neuron. 
These components coordinate to regulate muscle contraction 
through stretch reflexes and subsequent gamma motor neuron 
activity. Although increased alpha motor neuron activation leads 
to skeletal muscle contraction, decreased gamma motor neuron 
activity leads to muscle relaxation.
109 Of clinical note, when 
gamma motor neurons fail to maintain their inhibitory tone, 
tetanus results.
Golgi Tendon Reflex
Golgi tendon organs (GTO) are sensory mechanoreceptors lo -
cated within tendons and play a role in t he management of 
muscle tension (Fig. 17.2). They synapse with inhibitory inter-
neurons in the spinal cord, allowing for the inhibition of specific 
alpha motor neurons. Increased tension (stretch) in a skeletal

muscle inhibits alpha motor neurons to that muscle, which causes 
decreased firing of motor units. This resultant muscular relaxation 
with increased GTO activity functions to protect tendons; this is 
the opposite activity to that of the gamma motor neuron, which 
functions to prevent injury to muscles from overstretching 
through muscle contraction.
Muscle Stretch Reflexes
Muscle stretch reflexes, such as the patellar tendon reflex, are 
monosynaptic. With the knee flexed to 90 degrees, the quadriceps 
muscle is placed in a mild stretch. A sudden strike of a reflex ham-
mer against the tendon results in a dynamic stretch, stimulating 
the alpha motor neurons to contract the quadriceps muscle, ex-
tending the knee.
Spinal Facilitation
Spinal cord facilitation is maintenance of a pool of neurons in a 
state of subthreshold excitation. In this state, less afferent stimula-
tion is required to produce a response. Consider a model of a 
sound system with a microphone, amplifier, and speaker. Facilita-
tion acts as if the gain control on the amplifier is turned up. Given 
a normal input to the microphone, the speaker is too loud. 
 Degenhardt et al. have defined somatic dysfunction as “sites of 
muscle hypertonicity, tenderness, and joint restriction.”103 Spinal 
facilitation results in hyperactivity of both the general somatic 
system and the autonomic nervous system. When maintained 
chronically, facilitation may result in a state of central sensitiza-
tion, by which an “increased response of CNS neurons…inform[s] 
us of pain when faced with inputs coming from low threshold 
mechanoreceptors.”
257
Autonomic Activation
It is now understood that autonomic activation likely plays an 
important role in the clinical effects associated with osteopathic 
manipulative treatment (OMT). Early research studies on facilita-
tion demonstrate how behavior of the spinal cord is altered.
223 
This was accomplished when pressure was applied to spinous 
processes and was then measured to determine how much pres -
sure was necessary to produce an electromyographic response in 
the muscle. A facilitated segment requires less pressure to produce 
a response. The sympathetic nervous system innervates sweat 
glands (although this is a cholinergic response). Spinal cord fa -
cilitation results in increased sweating at the segmental level. 
Other factors affect spinal cord behavior.
150 It was demonstrated 
that the spinal cord has “memory” that results in conditioned re-
flexes. If a stimulus is maintained for a certain period, then re -
moval of the stimulus does not necessarily eliminate the response. 
When the mix of afferent input is altered, the cord becomes more 
sensitized to incoming signals. Afferent input from dysfunctional 
visceral structures produces viscerosomatic reflexes and facilita -
tion. This process may be implicated in certain chronic pain 
conditions. Studies have shown that nociception maintains facili-
tation.
352 A number of conditions, including fibromyalgia, rheu-
matoid arthritis, chronic low back pain, migraine, among others, 
have documented sensitization states.281 Animal studies have been 
conducted in which afferent fibers from the spindle to the cord 
were cut, and facilitation continued. Blocking nociceptive input 
tends to cause facilitation to disappear.
The previous discussion of neurophysiology only scratches the 
surface. The nervous system is not just complex, but dynamic 
(Fig. 17.3). There is a neuroendocrine immune system at work as 
well, connecting various physiologic functions to the presenting

make sure the muscle relaxes. The focus of the practitioner during 
treatment is to assess how the patient is responding to the treatment 
rather than whether the gamma gain has been reduced.
Descending Inhibition
Vigotsky and Bruhns looked at the role of descending inhibition 
in manual therapy, and they found that manipulation, mobiliza -
tion, and soft tissue therapies are partially mediated by the 
 analgesic effects attributable to descending inhibition. 354 We can 
define descending inhibition as endogenous analgesia, or the sum 
effects of the neurotransmitters that act on key structures in the 
brain to dampen nociceptive signaling from the body. Degen -
hardt et al. found that OMT increases beta endorphins and endo-
cannabinoids 30 minutes after treatment.102
Indications and Goals of Manual Medicine
Somatic dysfunction is the indication for manual medicine and is 
diagnosed by palpatory examination. If manual medicine is con -
sidered as a treatment option, there are two questions: Are there 
signs of a musculoskeletal component to the patient’s complaint? 
Does the somatic dysfunction found in the patient seem to be 
contributing to the patient’s complaint? If the answer to these 
questions is “yes,” then manual medicine may be considered as a 
potential treatment. Somatic dysfunction can coexist with ortho -
pedic and rheumatologic diseases.281 Manual medicine treatment 
helps the somatic dysfunction and helps the patient, but the un -
derlying disease process will remain. Other confounding factors 
may also be causes for the somatic dysfunction. The patient might 
have an anatomic short leg, which will continue to maintain sac -
roiliac and low back dysfunction. Certain activities might be 
stressful for the musculoskeletal system and challenge its current 
capacity. In a controlled study of low back pain, it is impossible 
to control for these confounding factors. Despite this, manual 
medicine may still be an appropriate option for conservative treat-
ment before more aggressive measures are taken.
Examination and Diagnosis for  
Manual Medicine
An examination is a process of data gathering. Subjective data can 
be obtained by taking a history. On taking a history from a pa -
tient, Max Gutensohn stated, “I always had a feeling that if I 
could talk to patients long enough, they would tell me what was 
wrong with them.”35 Pain, discomfort, or functional loss is a fre -
quent complaint. Additional information, such as predisposing 
factors, patient motivation, postural issues, and stresses, can be 
elicited from a good history. Physical examination includes 
 acquiring a sufficient physical examination database to enable 
 appropriate diagnosis and treatment. The musculoskeletal exami-
nation goes beyond the standard examination of looking for 
problems in a system. The practitioner should look for clues about 
the health status and function of the patient. Is there a somatic 
component to the patient’s problem? As practitioners of manual 
medicine, it is important to also appreciate that the therapeutic 
alliance between physician and patient helps establish trust, which 
is further reinforced through touch. This connection also has 
physiologic correlates that include the release of oxytocin. 354
The musculoskeletal screening examination looks at gait, pos -
ture, and symmetry or asymmetry. This is ordinarily done with 
the patient standing. A standardized modified 10-step screening 
exam by Greenman (see Table 17.1) can be used, or a 12-step 
biomechanical screening examination may be done. 105 Alterna -
tively, the screening examination may be nonstandard, with a 
systematic approach used to evaluate all body regions. This 
 examination can be integrated into a comprehensive physical  
examination. A standardized examination is ordinarily used by 
students, whereas experienced physicians often raise questions, 
and the examination is tailored to finding answers to the ques -
tions. For efficiency, the patient should be examined in multiple 
positions: standing, seated, supine, and prone. T o conserve time 
and enhance efficiency, all tests should be completed with the 
patient in one position before moving the patient to the next 
position.
Palpation for Tissue Texture Abnormality
Tissue texture abnormality is palpable evidence of physiologic 
dysfunction. The approach to palpation is to compare right vs. left 
and above vs. below. When evaluating a single area without com-
paring with adjacent areas, it is difficult to come to a meaningful 
conclusion. Description of dysfunctional areas is done relative to 
these adjacent areas. Palpation is done in layers, projecting your 
sense of touch to the depth required. Acute tissue texture change 
can be described and remembered by thinking of acute inflamma-
tion and the four cardinal signs: red, puffy, painful or tender, and 
warm. With acute tissue texture changes, sweating is increased, 
and the skin is usually moist (increased sympathetic tone). 
Chronic tissue texture abnormality is associated with thin, dry, 
atrophic skin that is cool. The palpatory quality is firm or fibrotic, 
and motion testing reveals motion loss.
Paraspinal viscerosomatic reflexes have palpatory qualities that 
are characteristic and give the experienced clinician clues that 
these changes may be a result of visceral disturbances. The maxi -
mum intensity of the findings is reported to be at the costotrans -
verse and rib angle areas. The greatest number of findings is in the 
skin and subcutaneous tissue. Palpating for tissue texture abnor -
malities can be an accurate and efficient method of identifying 
problem areas, as well as their acuity, in the musculoskeletal sys -
tem that require further examination.
Motion Testing
There are multiple methods for motion testing. Because manipu-
lative treatment has as its immediate objective the improvement 
of motion, motion testing skills and treatment skills become in -
tertwined, with motion testing being used for ongoing diagnosis 
throughout the treatment. Types of motion testing include:
• Observation of active motion
• Palpation of active motion with palpating fingers over the facet 
area

Assessment of Fascia
Fascia (Latin, “band” or “bandage”) has unique features that in -
clude the formation of sheets with multidirectional fibers giving 
it tensile strength, and sheets of collagen fibers and elastin that 
allow for shortening and elongating, thus accounting for its flex -
ibility.
213 The fascia may be considered exoskeletal, serving as a 
soft connective tissue anchor for muscles. 27 By contrast, there is 
little or no motion in scar tissue. Fascia is three dimensional and 
can form sleeves to compartmentalize, act as cables, or form dia -
phragms. All these properties must be considered when assessing 
fascia. Assessment of fascia starts with placement of the hands to 
perceive the combined vector force in the tissue.

In the assessment of a three-dimensional region, such as the

restricts deformation. Additionally, it is now understood that 
fascia does not permanently deform from mechanical stretch ap -

motion, pronation-supination).
Types of Manual Medicine Techniques
Overview
There are certain classifications of manual medicine techniques, 
including soft tissue technique, articulatory technique, or focused 
joint mobilization, which can be applied directly or indirectly. A 
layered approach when using combined technique is initiated 
with indirect technique facilitating a release in the tissues followed 
by direct technique. (Comprehensive descriptions of the most 
utilized techniques in manual medicine are described later.) 
 Although the research determining the successfulness of OMT on 
various conditions is limited, there is new evidence to emerge in 
the coming years as its popularity increases along with the aware -
ness of this field of medicine. Incorporating these techniques into

Direct Techniques
Soft Tissue Technique
Soft tissue technique addresses the muscular and fascial structures 
of the body as well as its associated neurovascular elements.
105 It 
is a direct, regional technique and requires experienced hands to 
monitor tissue feedback and appropriate response throughout 
treatment. Typically, they involve deep pressure, linear stretching, 
lateral stretching, traction, or any combination directed at sepa -
rating the origin and insertion of a muscle
72 (Fig. 17.6).
When performing soft tissue technique, it is important to 
move slowly into the restrictive barrier allowing for tissue relax -
ation before proceeding with increased force. Similarly, it is also 
important to allow for slow release of the applied force preventing 
tissues from snapping back quickly, which could elicit muscular 
spasm. The finger pads, thenar eminence, and hypothenar emi -
nence are used to apply force, avoiding direct pressure over bony 
prominences and sliding over the skin. The application of soft 
tissue technique can be modified in various ways to achieve treat-
ment goals. There are a variety of indications for soft tissue tech-
nique, including passive stretch of shortened myofascial struc -
tures, relaxation of hypertonic/spastic muscle, enhancement of 
circulation to myofascial tissues, movement of fluids, and prepa-
ration of periarticular soft tissues for more definitive and localized 
joint mobilization.
72,103,379
Articulatory Technique
The articulatory technique mobilizes joints, including facets (i.e., 
zygapophyseal intervertebral joints) with repetitive, slow move -
ments utilizing a combination of leverage, patient ventilatory 
movement, and a fulcrum to achieve mobilization of a restricted 
joint (Fig. 17.7). This is most often done by repeatedly applying 
a low-velocity, high-amplitude force to directly engage and then

Mobilization With Impulse (Thrust or High Velocity,  
Low Amplitude)
Mobilization with impulse, thrust maneuver, or HVLA technique 
is a direct form of manual medicine commonly prescribed for 
cervical, thoracic, lumbar, rib, sacroiliac, or extremity somatic 
dysfunction.
10,30,171,187,229 The physician positions the restricted 
joint toward the restrictive barrier and then delivers a quick thrust 
to affect the release of that joint.
174 There is often an audible “pop” 
that occurs when the restricted joint is released; however, the 
noise itself has no bearing on treatment outcome. Precise diagno-
sis of motion restriction is essential before application of this 
technique. Diagnosis must incorporate all three planes of motion: 
flexion-extension, side bending, and rotation.
72 After a precise 
segmental diagnosis has been made, preparation of surrounding 
periarticular tissues is performed. The physician then engages the 
restrictive barrier in all three planes; the more precise the localiza-
tion by positioning, the less thrusting force is required for treat -
ment (Figs. 17.8–17.10). Once the restrictive barrier is isolated 
and engaged, there is a momentary waiting period to allow for 
relaxation of the muscles in the area. During this time, the pa -
tient’s ventilatory cycle is monitored by the practitioner, and the 
treatment thrust is delivered at the point of deepest exhala -
tion.
72,105 The thrust must be low amplitude, meaning a very 
short distance, and high velocity (,0.5 sec). 174 HVLA forces 
should be avoided. Force that is not focused at the area of somatic

Understanding how segmental dysfunction occurs is complex 
and relies on the central segmental motor control (CSMC). It 
relies on input from both within the body (interoception) and 
outside the body (exteroception). There are internal body signals, 
such as proprioception, vestibular factors, vasomotor, immune, 
autonomic, and visceral distention of bladder, stomach, rectum, 
or esophagus. Exteroception is the perception of external envi -
ronmental stimuli, such as visual, auditory, touch, smell, and 
taste stimuli.
161 There are also anticipatory and postural control 
mechanisms that enable the accurate execution of this intended 
movement. There is actual sensory feedback that occurs com -
pared with expected feedback. If there is a problem with CSMC, 
this can lead to abnormal multisensory processing and filtering 
of interoceptive and exteroceptive stimuli. Over time, this can 
result in poor motor control of the vertebral column as well as 
other muscles in the body. Ultimately, this can lead to ongoing 
maladaptive changes and, with repeated microtraumas, can be 
responsible for the development of musculoskeletal pain syn-
dromes. However, this model also explains how spinal adjust -
ments that are delivered to segments with a CSMC problem can 
improve vertebral column motor control through the CNS with 
mechanoreceptive input from the segment with a CSMC prob -
lem. This needs proprioceptive input from the deep paraspinal 
muscles. Over time, these changes in the awareness of the CNS 
are thought to lead to maladaptive changes in neural function 
and in body structure and function, worsening its ability to adapt 
and respond to internal and environmental cues, thus impairing 
ideal motor control and causing a variety of symptoms, diseases, 
and disorders. By doing so, they activate or improve the function 
of these dysfunctional deep paraspinal muscles. Other studies 
have confirmed that the speed of a corrective mobilization greatly 
impacts muscle spindle responses during a manipulation and

Muscle Energy
Muscle energy (ME) technique was first introduced by Fred 
Mitchell Sr. This technique is a form of treatment in which vol -
untary muscular contraction by the patient (isometric, concen-
tric, or eccentric), from a precisely controlled position, is executed 
against a distinct counterforce provided by the practitio -
ner.
258,259,260,261 ME is an active technique that can be applied to 
most muscle groups in the body or any joint that is crossed by 
voluntary muscle. ME can be used to reposition or mobilize a 
joint to restore its normal ROM, lengthen a spastic/shortened 
muscle, strengthen a physiologically weak muscle or muscle 
group, or decrease edema and lymphatic congestion.
56,135,259–261 
Most ME utilizes a direct, isometric contraction of the patient as 
the final activating force. ME requires a specific diagnosis in  
all three planes of motion. The practitioner then engages the re -
strictive barrier in all three planes to the point d escribed by 
Mitchell Sr. as the “feather edge,” or where resistance is first felt 
by the practitioner.
258–261 The patient is then instructed to provide 
an equal counterforce away from the restrictive barrier against the 
resistance applied by the practitioner ( Fig. 17.11). This isometric 
counterforce is held for 3 to 5 seconds, followed by complete

the patient under the ischial tuberosity providing a rotational force poste -

motion. This process is repeated three to five times to lengthen

and 17.9).
ME techniques are effective in improving reported pain, dis -
ability, and joint ROM in both asymptomatic and symptomatic 
patients. The studies evaluated in this review have provided evi -
dence that MEs are specifically effective for alleviating chronic 
pain of the lower back and neck and chronic lateral epicondylitis. 
There is also evidence supporting MEs as a beneficial therapy for 
reducing acute lower back pain and improving the related dis -
ability indexes. However, further evidence is needed to confirm 
MEs as effective treatment for plantar fasciitis and other muscu -
loskeletal disorders.
339 There are very few contraindications to 
ME, and it is considered a relatively safe technique because the 
patient controls the corrective force being employed. Common 
errors when performing ME include inaccurate diagnosis, failure 
to properly engage the barrier, erroneous muscle contraction by 
the patient (i.e., too much force, the wrong duration, or too short 
duration), and not allowing enough time for postisometric relax -
ation to occur before repositioning the patient to a new restrictive 
barrier. ME can be used effectively even by novice practitioners 
and can be learned by patients as part of a home exercise program 
to treat somatic dysfunction in nearly any area of the body.
Direct Myofascial Release
MFR is an osteopathic technique used to treat somatic dysfunc-
tion involving myofascial tissues and supportive structures. Fascia 
is a contiguous system throughout the body with blood supply, 
fluid drainage, and neurologic innervation, thus comprising one 
of the largest organ systems in the body.
72 Direct MFR utilizes a 
combination of traction, compression, and twisting maneuvers to 
stack all available planes of myofascial tissues into a restrictive 
barrier (Fig. 17.12).
105,300 Myofascial tissues under a sustained 
load will elongate secondary to the viscoelastic properties of col -
lagen, referred to as “creep,” and the practitioner will appreciate a 
release of the tissues under a sensing hand.
72 The release of the 
tissues may take several seconds to minutes and is continuously 
followed by the practitioner toward increasing compliance until 
no further relaxation is felt. This release might be likened to melt-
ing an ice cube (Videos 17.10 and 17.11). This technique may 
also be applied indirectly by stacking tissue planes into the posi -
tion of ease away from the restrictive barrier, which may provide 
better tolerance for the patient. MFR can be performed on a va -
riety of structures, including fascia, tendons, scars, internal or -
gans, and visceral suspensory ligaments.
72,251,354 Direct MFR must 
be applied with caution when immobilization of tissues is critical 
for proper healing, and continued palpatory assessment is impor -
tant by the practitioner to adjust the MFR procedure accordingly 
to achieve the desired effect.

or ease to promote pain relief, relengthen contracted or hyper -

held for 90 seconds, or 120 seconds for rib tender points. As the 
tissues reset, the practitioner may feel a “therapeutic pulse” at the 
site of the tender point. After time is completed, the patient is

Counterstrain can be applied to a wide range of patient popu-

contraindications to this technique. A limiting factor to the coun-
terstrain technique is the time required to release each tender 
point; therefore patients with diffuse tender points may benefit 
from alternative techniques. Patients should be educated about 
possible soreness after this technique is performed and encour -

restriction and is a very good starting point for those learning 
manual medicine techniques.
Craniosacral Technique
Craniosacral osteopathy technique was developed by William 
Garner Sutherland for diagnosis and treatment of the body by 
way of the primary respiratory mechanism.
171 Sutherland per-
ceived that the cranial bones undergo subtle cyclical motions 
about the cranial sutures. This motion is most easily appreciated 
at the cranium and sacrum but is also palpable throughout the 
body. Sutherland termed this inherent motion the “primary respi-
ratory mechanism,” which is comprised of five components:  
(1) the articular mechanism of the cranial bones, (2) involuntary 
motion of the sacrum between the ilia, (3) fluctuation of CSF ,  
(4) reciprocal tension of dural membranes, and (5) inherent  
motion of the CNS.
128,171,248 Craniosacral technique relies on  
the assessment of amplitude, rate, symmetry, and the quality of 
the primary respiratory mechanism. Gentle pressure is often ap -
plied indirectly in rhythm with the palpated inherent motion to 
passively move toward optimal mobility. This technique can also 
be applied directly, such as in the setting of sutural impactions. 
Craniosacral technique has vast applications and is commonly 
used for infantile colic, fibromyalgia, chronic headaches, postcon-
cussive syndrome, or following head trauma.
14,166,192,193
Other Indirect and Combined Techniques
There are several other osteopathic techniques utilized by practi -
tioners to treat somatic dysfunction throughout the body. Indirect 
techniques include functional technique, balanced ligamentous 
tension, and facilitated positional release (Fig. 17.14). Combined 
technique, synonymous with the Still technique, begins with in -
direct technique and finishes with direct technique. Theoretically, 
these techniques primarily rely on the neurologic model of 
 treatment to produce a release in areas of diagnosed somatic dys -
function. These techniques can be challenging for the novice 
practitioner to utilize; however, when a release occurs, it is very 
apparent to the treating clinician because there is a decrease of 
overall tension surrounding the area of dysfunction as well as 
improved ROM depending on the focus of treatment.
Clinical Use of Manual Medicine
The philosophic treatment goals of PM&R are to improve func -
tion and quality of life, which directly align with the goals of 
manual medicine. There is a wide scope of clinical applications of 
manual medicine to restore motion, promote symmetry, correct 
postural imbalances, decrease pain, and enhance overall quality of 
life for the patient. T echniques can be easily incorporated into the 
office visit after the examination, or patients may be referred to a 
practitioner specializing in manual medicine. An additional ad -
vantage of some forms of manual treatment is the ability to incor-
porate certain techniques into a home exercise program, notably 
ME and counterstrain, empowering the patient in lasting self-  
directed care and promoting an active treatment process.
T reatment plans for all types of manual medicine must be de-
veloped on an individual basis correlating to the structural diag -
nosis, the type and location of somatic dysfunction, techniques 
being performed, and the patient’s response to treatment. These 
parameters are continually reassessed by the practitioner to deter-
mine the need for continued manipulative treatment. As with 
most types of treatment for musculoskeletal pain and dysfunc -
tion, manual medicine is just one component of a comprehensive 
treatment plan.
45,175
Evidence-Based Use of Manual Medicine
The efficacy of manual medicine, as well as its associated risks, 
continue to generate controversy in the medical community. 
 Although research has strived to establish clinical guidelines and 
evidence-based literature supporting the use of manual medicine, 
conclusions are still limited given the inherent challenges in ade-
quately performing randomized controlled trials. Available studies 
are limited by heterogeneity of patients, the individual nature of 
diagnosis, lack of widely accepted or validated clinical outcome 
measures, and the difficulty of “blinding” patients to treatment. 
Despite these challenges, recent reviews of manual medicine indi-
cate effectiveness in certain populations. Available studies in 
neonatal settings provide evidence that manual medicine is effec -
tive in reducing hospital length of stay of the treated infant.
19,230 
In the setting of acute and chronic nonspecific low back pain, 
patients receiving manual medicine have increased functional 
status, higher satisfaction with treatment, and decreased overall 
pain.
85,134,329 There is also evidence some manual treatment 
 techniques may be more cost effective than usual primary care

Efficacy and Safety of Osteopathic  
Manipulative Therapy
Osteopathic manipulative medicine and other forms of manual 
medicine can be effective in the management of musculoskeletal 
disorders. In contrast, inconclusive evidence was derived from 
analyzing the efficacy on pediatric conditions, primary headache, 
and irritable bowel syndrome. Although not all randomized con -
trolled trials about this treatment have investigated the safety of 
manual medicine, considering that no serious adverse events have 
been reported, manual techniques can be considered safe. How -
ever, it should be considered that this is based on low numbers of 
studies, some of which are of only moderate quality. Therefore 
ongoing, further robust, well-defined research studies are needed.20 
Spinal manipulation/mobilization (SM) is effective in adults for 
acute, subacute, and chronic low back pain; migraine and cervi -
cogenic headache; and cervicogenic dizziness. Moderate- and 
high-quality evidence exists for acute and chronic low back pain, 
acute/subacute neck pain, acute whiplash, extremity conditions, 
and chronic neck pain. The evidence is inconclusive for cervical 
manipulation/mobilization alone for neck pain of any duration 
and for any type of manipulation/mobilization for midback pain, 
sciatica, tension-type headache, coccydynia, temporomandibular 
joint disorders, fibromyalgia, premenstrual syndrome, and pneu -
monia in older adults. Review of the current basic and clinical 
evidence highlights an additional issue. Much of the evidence 
base, both basic and clinical, is predicated upon short-term obser-
vations. With very few exceptions, basic research studies examine 
exposure to risk factors or therapeutic interventions that are pres-
ent for only hours or, at best, days. Similarly, clinical studies fre -
quently examine outcomes after only a single spinal mobilization 
or a course of therapy that is much shorter than that administered 
in clinical practice. It is also extremely important to consider on -
going or maintenance care after resolving the acute issue.75 Main-
tenance care is commonly questioned by patients, third-party 
payers, and the wider health care community.
The chiropractic perspective is that many individuals suffer 
from chronic health conditions for which there is no cure, but 
these patients can be maintained with minimal pain and disability 
through regular, periodic spinal mobilization treatment. It is esti-
mated that 10% to 19% of patients will develop chronic disabling 
neck or back pain. 75 T wo studies have examined chiropractic 
maintenance care. Descarreaux et al. studied 30 patients with 
chronic nonspecific low back pain (chronic defined as 6 months 
duration).101 Half were given 12 SM treatments in an intensive 
1-month period and no treatment for a 9-month follow-up pe -
riod. The other half were given the same intensive 1-month period 
of SM, but in the subsequent 9 months they received a mainte -
nance SM every 3 weeks. Pain and disability were monitored via 
a visual analog pain score and a modified Oswestry questionnaire. 
Both groups reported improved pain scores at the end of the 
 intensive SM period and maintained those scores over the subse -
quent 9 months. Although both groups also reported improved 
Oswestry disability scores at the end of the intensive treatment 
period, only the group receiving maintenance care during the 
9-month follow-up period maintained that improvement.  Patients 
not receiving spinal mobilization maintenance care reverted to 
pretreatment disability levels over the follow-up period. More re -
cently, a prospective, single-blinded, placebo-controlled study by 
Senna et al. was conducted to examine the long-term (10-month) 
effectiveness of lumbar SM with and without SM maintenance 
care for the treatment of chronic, nonspecific low back pain. 340 
Study participants reported significantly reduced pain and dis -
ability scores in the spinal mobilization study groups compared 
with the sham mobilization group at 1 month. However, only 
study participants receiving initial spinal mobilization followed by 
mobilization maintenance care showed improvement in pain and 
disability scores at the 10-month evaluation. By contrast, pain 
and disability scores were at pretreatment levels for study partici -
pants in the group without maintenance care and the sham 
groups at 10 months. These investigators concluded that spinal 
mobilization was effective as a treatment for chronic nonspecific 
low back pain, but to obtain long-term benefits a course of spinal 
mobilization maintenance care may be required.171
There is moderate-quality evidence that manipulation (i.e., 
thrust) interventions may produce small to moderate reduction in 
pain intensity compared with other forms of treatment, such as 
exercise. Thrust interventions are also likely to reduce disability for 
patients compared with other interventions. The effect seems to 
increase over time at 3- and 6-month follow-ups. There is moder-
ate-quality evidence that mobilization (i.e., nonthrust) interven -
tions are likely to have minimal effect compared with other active 
comparators in terms of reducing pain intensity or disability.85
Adverse events associated with manual treatment can be classi-
fied into two categories: (1) benign, minor, or nonserious and  
(2) serious. Generally, those that are benign are transient in na -
ture, mild to moderate in intensity, have little effect on activities, 
and are usually short lasting. Most commonly, these involve pain 
or discomfort to the musculoskeletal system. Less commonly, 
nausea, dizziness, or tiredness is reported. Serious adverse events 
are disabling, require hospitalization, and may be life threatening. 
The most documented and discussed serious adverse event associ-
ated with SM in cases of cervical spine mobilization is vertebro -
basilar artery stroke.20
Contraindications and Side Effects for 
Manual Medicine
Manual medicine techniques should only be used in patients with 
diagnosed somatic dysfunction. The practitioner must assess the 
benefit vs. potential risk when considering using manual medi -
cine in the overall treatment plan. Each type of technique has its 
own set of absolute and relative contraindications; however, mo -
bilization with impulse (HVLA) has the greatest number of con -
traindications ( Box 17.1 ), which should be carefully reviewed 
prior to treatment to prevent associated complications.
Adverse effects of manual medicine are rare but may include 
cauda equina syndrome, lumbar disc herniation, fracture, hema -
toma, or hemorrhagic cyst formation.197 Stroke and cervical arte-
rial dissection (CAD) associated with cervical spine manipulation 
and HVLA are perhaps the most concerning complications 
 debated within the field of manual medicine. There have been

might be at risk for vascular injury; however, these tools have 
controversial validity.188,305 Case reports of CAD associated with 
cervical spine manipulation have been reported in the literature 
since the early twentieth century, but to avoid unreasonable 
alarmism it is important to put this risk into proper perspective. 
First, the annual incidence of spontaneous internal CAD is esti -
mated to be 2.5 to 3 per 100,000; spontaneous vertebral artery 
dissection is estimated to be 1 to 1.5 per 100,000. 310 Second, es-

if not substantially less than, spontaneous CAD estimates, rang -

spine with improper technique, misdiagnosis, or both. Although 
these cases may provide the basis for opposing the use of manual 
medicine, it should be noted these catastrophic clinical conse -

diagnosis, and application of technique.7,305,340
Patients should be educated about potential side effects of 
manual medicine that may occur even with successful treatment. 
These include increased autonomic effects (hypotension, perspira-
tion, etc.) and a transient potential increase in discomfort. Post -
treatment pain and increased symptoms can occur several hours 
after treatment cessation. These subside spontaneously, and the 
patient is usually much improved after the rebound symptoms 
subside. There is also a potential of overdose of manual treatment, 
and the amount should be limited according to patient tolerance 
and response to treatment. Experience in treating patients with 
manual medicine is helpful and essential in making these clinical 
judgments.
Traction
Definition
T raction is a technique used to stretch soft tissues and to separate 
joint surfaces or bone fragments by use of a pulling force. 176  
The force applied must be of sufficient magnitude and duration 
in the proper direction while resisting movement of the body with 
an equal and opposite force.
Historical Perspective
Centuries ago, Hippocrates recommended the use of traction for 
scoliosis, kyphosis, and fractures of the femur. 176,182 Although 
new surgical techniques, hardware, and treatment strategies have 
altered the role of traction in the past few decades, it is commonly 
used to reduce other fractures and dislocations in the acute setting 
and has an ongoing role in the management of orthopedic 
trauma. The use of traction to treat outpatient spinal problems, 
such as cervical and lumbar disc disorders, became more wide -
spread after the 1950s. Initially, this was popularized by Cyriax for 
the proposed treatment of lumbar disc disorders.89,92 He hypoth-
esized that patients should be in constant traction for at least 
20 minutes to produce stretch and decompression of spinal struc -
tures. Judovich then identified that higher forces were poorly 
tolerated with constant traction and that the greater forces needed 
to overcome surface resistance were best used with intermittent 
traction.201-203 In the 1960s, Colachis and Strohm studied the 
biomechanics of traction and attempted to define the optimal 
angle of pull for cervical traction as well as the optimal duration 
of traction for both the lumbar and the cervical regions.79-82 They 
concluded that 24 degrees of flexion intermittently for 25  minutes 
produced optimal cervical distraction. This knowledge was com -
bined with the earlier identification by Judovich that 20 to 
25 pounds of force were required to produce distraction of the 
cervical spine segments.201
Contemporary studies regarding structural changes, optimal 
angle of pull, duration, and forces needed for treatment, especially 
in the lumbar spine, have produced varying results and  
have not clearly defined how best to deliver lumbar trac -
tion.31,74,76,126,212,262,338,362 When traction is applied in lumbar disc 
herniation, studies suggest pain reduction and possibly functional 
improvements in the short term compared with sham, but not in 
the long term. Despite this, traction continues to be a clinically 
popular treatment of cervical and lumbar pain disorders, and 
there continue to be more attempts and need for advances in the 
lumbar region. These have included motorized systems, stabiliz -
ing tables, and inversion techniques. 146,147,295,302,320,237,365 Many 
of these developments have occurred because of the difficulties in 
trying to effectively apply traction to the lumbar region. 295
Types of Traction
T raction can be delivered by a continuous, sustained, or intermit-
tent force. Continuous traction uses a low force over a long pe -
riod, such as 30 to 40 hours. 176 Continuous traction is more 
typically used for fracture management or spinal stabilization and 
is not very well tolerated for outpatient spine treatment. Sustained 
traction uses a larger force but for a shorter time period (typically 
30–60 minutes).176 Sustained traction is still difficult to tolerate 
but is often used in the lumbar spine with a split traction or au -
totraction table. 147,176,302,337 Intermittent traction uses greater 
forces over shorter periods.176 The traction force can be increased 
or decreased during each treatment cycle, and the duration of pull 
can be adjusted. The cycle is usually repeated for 15 to 25 min -
utes, with the traction phase ranging from 5 to 60 seconds, and 
the rest phase ranging from 5 to 15 seconds. 79,80 The magnitude, 
duration, and direction of the pull can be varied.

Cervical traction is commonly performed manually, mechani -

posterior distraction unit. 163,176 The optimal angle of pull ranges 
between 20 and 30 degrees of flexion, while 25 pounds of force 
is required to reverse the normal cervical lordosis and bring about 
the earliest distraction of vertebral segments.
79,90,202 Mechanical 
cervical traction can be applied in the supine position, which 
reduces the weight of the head but increases the frictional resis -
tance.
365 This position also allows for better control of the head 
by the patient, is typically more comfortable, and is likely more 
effective.
216 T raction in the sitting position allows more accurate 
positioning for the correct angle of pull, but usually this affords

can be used at home with a traditional over-the-door unit

Lumbar traction requires significantly greater forces to create 
distraction of the vertebral segments than cervical traction. Com-
mon traction systems include a thoracic or chest belt with a pelvic 
belt (Fig. 17.17), on a split traction table or an autotraction ta -

rests on the mobile half, which separates from the stationary 
portion. This allows the force necessary to overcome resistance 
and to produce distraction to be reduced.

There have been many other treatment devices developed in 
the last few decades, such as the Vertebral Axial Decompression 
(VAX-D), the SpinalAid system, and the DRX9000. The descrip-
tion of these devices uses the term decompression more than trac-

spine pain with or without leg pain. The use of the term decom-
pression is meant to separate these units from more traditional 
traction units and to imply that this type of distraction works 
more by creating a negative discal pressure, which is true.
295 How-
ever, the hypothesized theory that this pulls a disc back into its 
usual position is not confirmed. The number of sessions and the 
positioning for treatment with these devices are more well defined 
than for other types of traction, but this is based on practice pat -
terns more than on efficacy data or scientific investigation. Besides 
the use of these complex machines in a therapy office, there is 
continued interest in trying to develop home units for lumbar 
traction and/or decompression. Most distracting braces and home 
units developed have not been tested in clinical trials, and the 
only confirmed beneficial effects published for traction have been 
from inversion tables.

Physiologic Effects, Indications, and Efficacy
The physiologic effects of traction have been extensively evaluated 
and reported. T raction can stretch muscles and ligaments, tighten 
the posterior longitudinal ligament to exert a centripetal force on 
the annulus fibrosis, enlarge the intervertebral space, enlarge the 
intervertebral foramina, and separate apophyseal joints in the  
spine.
79–82,155,176 It naturally follows that indications for spinal 
traction include conditions in which these physiologic effects 
would be deemed beneficial, but there are no clearly definitive 
studies to support its use. The condition having the most support 
for its use is cervical radiculopathy.
44,137,216,219,304,307 The early lit-
erature  supporting this clinical practice was limited to two case 
series suggesting good resolution of symptoms, but more recent 
trials and reviews have increased the level of support and known 
 benefit.44,83,137,216,263,304 However, the demonstration of more 
 effectiveness with cervical traction is often combined with the use 
of other physical modalities, including exercise, and the long-term

The use of traction for axial neck pain is less well established. 
Articles reviewing this topic have concluded that there is insuffi -
cient evidence to determine the efficacy of traction in treating 
cervical pain without arm pain.
155,183,373 Home traction units have 
not established any superiority to usual therapy for neck pain, but 
there have not been any studies comparing home traction units to 
placebo or no treatment. As with many other diagnoses, there has 
been a proposed prediction rule to determine who may be a re-
sponder to cervical traction, but this has not been validated.
48,297 
Clinically, if there is no contraindication (see later), a trial of 
cervical traction may be attempted in any person with neck pain 
or neck and arm pain who may benefit from the known physio -
logic effects, and small studies suggest some potential benefit. This 
practice pattern to determine efficacy would be acceptable in the 
cervical spine because home units are available to potentially trial. 
If effective, the use of a home unit for treatment can provide a 
cost-effective approach with significant independence and self-
management for persons with chronic cervical problems.
The popularity of lumbar traction in treating low back pain, 
with or without radicular features, has declined over the past few 
decades.
106 This change came about because of a lack of literature 
support for the treatment and an emphasis on more active treat -
ment programs in the lumbar spine. In the late 1980s, clinical 
interest was sparked by a case series of 49 patients with sciatica of 
greater than 6-week duration. Gillstrom found 79% improve-
ment with traction in this population, but the study was uncon-
trolled.
147 The next studies compared traction with a sham 
 treatment or traction plus physical therapy with physical therapy 
alone.29,38 Both studies found no difference in outcome between 
the treatment groups. More recent trials have looked specifically 
at patients with disc herniation, disc herniation with nerve com -
pression/leg pain, or mechanical or chronic back pain comparing 
different forms of traction or traction to other ther -
apy.
31,73,104,107,209,212,288,291,338 A large trial by Thackeray et al. in 
2016 randomized 120 subjects with back and leg pain from disc 
compression to standard extension-based physical therapy for 
6  weeks with or without mechanical traction. They concluded 
that there is no evidence that adding the mechanical traction to a 
standard extension-based exercise program produces any superior-
ity in benefit.
338
Clinically, it is still felt that the current techniques are limited 
in their ability to apply the forces directly to the lumbar spine 
segments. Real-time magnetic resonance imaging during sus -

structurally are significantly lower in body weight, shorter in 
stature, and require lower traction force to body weight. With 
these clinical difficulties for performing lumbar traction, inver -
sion traction units have much interest and are used more fre-
quently in home settings. Gianakopoulos et al. initially studied 
this in 1985 and found improvement in 13 of 16 patients with 
low back pain treated with an inversion device.

paring decompression vs. traction, Demirel et al. report decom -
pression to be more effective, but this was measured by the disc 
herniation index, a little used tool, whereas Choi et al. note equal 
improvement in pain disability and straight-leg raise for persons 
with disc herniation. 73,104 Demeril et al. report similar improve -
ment in pain, functional restoration, and thickness of disc hernia-
tion with lumbar stabilization with or without decompression. 104
Contraindications
Absolute contraindications to traction include malignancy, infec-
tion (e.g., osteomyelitis, discitis), osteoporosis, inflammatory 
 arthritis, fracture, pregnancy, cord compression, uncontrolled 
hypertension, or cardiovascular disease as well as in the setting of 
carotid or vertebral artery disease. 176,202 Most practitioners are in 
agreement that traction should be discontinued if there is any 
exacerbation of symptoms, discomfort from the traction device, 
or with the production of systemic symptoms, such as dizziness in 
cervical traction. Inversion traction involves more risks because 
increases in blood pressure and decreases in heart rate are known 
to occur, leading to headaches and periorbital petechiae.146 There 
should be caution when using traction in the elderly, in the setting 
of a midline disc herniation, and in the lumbar region when ab -
dominal problems are present. Although a trial of cervical or 
lumbar traction is generally tolerated if there are none of the 
above contraindications, the definitive efficacy is low.
Massage
Definition
Massage is the term used to describe certain soft tissue manipula-
tions of the body done with the hands; it includes friction, knead-
ing, rolling, and percussion techniques to the external tissues of 
the body. Massage is administered for the purpose of producing 
effects on the nervous system, muscular system, and general or 
local circulation of the blood and lymph.24,42,55,71,86,141,151,241,265,317 
This may be done in a variety of ways, with a curative, palliative, 
preventative, or hygienic objective in view.87,129,241,253,311 Massage 
and manipulation have common historical roots, and in early 
descriptions of the two techniques the words are used inter -
changeably. More recently, they have been separated into two 
distinct modalities, but they continue to share similar terminol -
ogy, philosophy, and technique while being delivered by a variety 
of practitioners.42,55,86,150
History
Massage has waxed and waned in popularity throughout millen -
nia in different cultures. The postulated origins of this word 
 include the Arabic verb massa, which means “to touch”; the Greek 
word massein, meaning “to knead”; and the Sanskrit term makek, 
which means “to press or condense.” 208,215 All these terms were 
derived at different times and in different cultures, depicting the 
significant role that massage played in many societies. The earliest 
known reference to massage is in cave paintings in the Pyrenees 
that date back to 15,000 BCE and depict the use of hands for 
therapeutic touch. 84 There are also ancient medical records 
 depicting massage as a therapeutic intervention from early China 
(2700 BCE), India (1500–1200 BCE), and Babylon (900 BCE).
The ancient Greek and Roman medical literature makes 
 abundant references to the use of massage as a therapeutic modal-
ity. Hippocrates wrote extensively on the use of massage in his 
book, On Articulations .208 Plato (427–347 BCE) and Socrates 
(470–399 BCE) made reference to anointing with oils when per -
forming massage. Asclepiades (129–40 BCE), a Greek physician 
who practiced in Rome and is considered the father of physical 
medicine, wrote about hydrotherapy, exercise, and massage as the 
three most important therapeutic modalities to use in the treat -
ment of patients.208
In the early Middle Ages (AD 500–1500), the Church of 
Rome discouraged the use of massage as a healing practice, so it 
fell out of favor. During the Renaissance (AD 1300–1600), 
medical scholars resurrected massage and attempted to under -
stand the physiology and anatomy this modality affected. The 
French medical community embraced “friction of the skin” and 
described many different techniques for providing massage. Many 
of the terms for these techniques, such as effleurage, petrissage, 
tapotement, and friction massage, are still in use today.150,208
The roots of modern-day massage are derived from the work of 
Per Henrik Ling (AD 1776–1839) of Sweden. 208 He was the 
founder of curative gymnastics, an approach that incorporated 
massage and exercise and used many of the previously described 
French techniques and nomenclature. He also used terms such as 
rolling, sliding, pinching, shaking, and vibration to describe his 
massage techniques. Ling was instrumental in establishing the 
Central Royal Institute for Gymnastics, which practiced, taught, 
and promoted what has become known as Swedish massage. Sev-
eral of Ling’s students immigrated to New York and established 
the Swedish Institute of Massage in 1916.208
During the 1900s, Mennel and Cyriax strongly advocated 
the use of massage in their orthopedic practices. Mennell wrote 
Physical Treatment by Movements, Manipulation, and Massage  in 
1917. This book greatly influenced the field of physical therapy 
to use massage as a therapeutic modality. Cyriax wrote exten -
sively on the use of manipulation and deep friction massage, 91 
which today is used for the treatment of spine, musculoskeletal, 
and sports injuries. The popularity of massage as a therapeutic 
intervention has continued to fluctuate over the past several 
decades, but the recent resurgence of interest in massage has 
generated new and increased scientific inquiry into its 
 usage.3,15,40,78,88,120,125,129,141,165,169,172,196,226,239,241,253,265,284,341,342,343
Indications and Goals of Treatment
Massage has multiple effects on the body, including mechanical, 
reflexive, neurologic, and psychological.2,28,33,60,236,237,239,241,253,332, 
349,356,357,363,368-370 The exact therapeutic mechanism by which 
massage works for specific diagnoses is not fully understood and 
probably represents a combination of the previously mentioned 
ideas. The magnitude of these effects is variable depending on  
the type and application of the various techniques. The goals of

therapeutic massage are to produce relaxation, relieve muscle ten-
sion, reduce pain, increase mobility of soft tissues, reduce edema, 
and improve circulation. These techniques can be used on patients 
with any diagnosis who would benefit from changes in the tissues 
and/or reduction of swelling and discomfort. There are also benefits 
to massage for patients with cancer diagnoses,
33,113,239 reducing the 
need for additional pain-relieving medications,3,40,55,86,121,196,227,242,364 
reducing chemotherapy-related side effects such as nausea and
