lymphocytes to fight tumors.172
Mechanical and Physiologic Effects
The mechanical effects of massage are the most apparent and 
 understood. These effects are brought on by the physical forces 
applied to the body tissues, such as compression, shearing, or vi -
bration. The mechanical pressure created by massage moves fluid 
from areas of relative stasis (low pressure) to higher pressure areas 
by creating a hydrostatic pressure gradient. Once fluids leave the 
cells or interstitial areas, they can enter the lymphatic or vascular 
system. Valves within the lymphatic and venous system prevent 
return of the fluid to the tissue.
21,53,221,241,303,341 Massage can have 
an immediate effect on cutaneous blood flow, with hyperemia 
being noticed even with superficial techniques.
150 The mechanism 
of action for this is not well understood, but it is most likely a 
combination of increased arteriolar pressure
241,361 and stimulation 
of the mast cells with release of histamines. This local histamine 
release may lead to redness or the formation of dermatographia at 
the site of stimulation.
Deep massage has an effect on the underlying fascia and deep 
connective tissues.
120,207,217,376 Injury to this deeper tissue can re-
sult in restrictions, adhesions, and scarring. These fascial constric-
tions can potentially cause restriction of fluid movement within 
the vasculature as well as reduction in muscle activity and move -
ment. Deep massage can help to release these restrictions, adhe -
sions, and microscarring.
62,151,207,217,376
Pain, inactivity, and restriction of movement result in insuf-
ficient muscle activation to mobilize fluids. 255,341,343,347,370 This 
 hypomobility can result in increased fluid stasis, producing a 
self-perpetuating feedback loop. This can result in fluid accu -
mulation as well as accumulation of metabolic byproducts 
such as lactate dehydrogenase, aspartate transaminase, tumor 
necrosis factor alpha, and other inflammatory cytokines.
54,241,311 
These metabolic byproducts can create an osmotic influence on 
fluid shifts and result in stimulation of pain fibers. Massage 
increases the mobility of these metabolic byproducts and the 
dispersion of fluid accumulations.
87,311 Once this self-perpetu-
ating cycle of pain, stasis, and hypomobility is broken, the 
body can restore its normal healing mechanism, including 
regulation of glucose uptake, protein synthesis, and immune 
cell  activ ation. 52,58,160,241
It has been postulated that massage also results in neural reflex 
reactions.224,281 It is thought that these reactions are what account 
for the more global effects produced with massage techniques. 
Somatic afferent nerve fibers carry information from the somatic 
system to the spinal cord. Dysfunction within the somatic struc-
ture can result in increased afferent neural input. This increased 
input results in a change in the efferent activity at the same spinal 
cord level through interneurons and can result in muscle hyperto-
nicity and contraction. This hypertonic region can localize to a 
specific spinal cord level and has been considered by many to be 
a facilitative spinal segment. This would be demonstrated in the 
effects that can occur in persons who have experienced  complete108 
and incomplete spinal cord injuries.250
Types of Massage
A vast array of techniques has been used to perform therapeutic 
massage. These techniques can be categorized by geographic re -
gion of origin, either classic Western (European) or Eastern 
(Asian) forms of massage. The most common Western techniques 
are those outlined by the Swedish system.
24 These four basic mas-
sage strokes are effleurage, petrissage, friction massage, and ta-
potement (as originally described by the French).
38,150,208 Several 
treatment schemes combine these massage principles with other 
movement therapies to enhance structural reintegration and func-
tional restoration. The most common Eastern massage techniques 
include acupressure, anma, reflexology, shiatsu, Thai massage, and 
tuina.
Western Forms of Massage
Effleurage. Effleurage involves gliding the palms, fingertips, 
and/or thumbs over the skin in a rhythmic circular pattern with 
varying degrees of pressure.
24,91,142,179 This stroke is performed by 
maintaining continuous contact with the skin and stroking from 
a distal to proximal position on the extremities, torso, or spine 
(Fig. 17.18). Once the proximal position is reached, the hands 
can be gently dragged over the skin and repositioned over the 
distal position and the process repeated. This technique is often 
used as a prelude to more aggressive massage techniques or ma -
nipulation. Effleurage performed superficially can result in reflex-
ive and psychological changes. The blood flow to the area is 
 increased, introducing relaxation when done slowly and stimula -
tion when done more quickly. Deeper mechanical stroking can 
result in mechanical effects on the circulatory and deep myofascial 
systems. Effleurage is used to stimulate lymphatic drainage and to 
relieve joint sprain–type pain, muscle strains, and bruising, as well 
as vascular congestion related to surgery, peripheral vascular dis-
ease, or possibly complex regional pain syndrome.
2,142,247,350
Petrissage. Petrissage is also known as kneading massage. It 
involves both hands compressing the skin between the thumb and 
fingers.
91,156,179 The tissue is grasped from the underlying skeletal 
structures, lifted, and massaged. Both hands alternate rhythmically 
in a rolling motion. The depth of petrissage can determine the

mechanical effect. Superficial techniques promote relaxation, 
whereas deeper techniques increase blood flow, mobilize fluid and 
tissue deposits, decrease adhesions, and increase tissue pliability. 
Petrissage is also considered compression massage, and several 
variations exist, including kneading or picking up, wringing, roll-
ing, or shaking the tissue. Kneading involves circular movements 
of one hand superimposed on the other. The finger pads and 
thumb compress tissue and distract it from the deeper underlying 
structures. Picking up is a technique that involves four basic steps: 
(1) compression of the soft tissue against the underlying structures, 
(2) grasping the soft tissue, (3) compression, and (4) release, then 
repositioning the hands in a more proximal position to repeat the 
process. Wringing resembles picking up, except that once tissue is 
grasped, one hand pushes while the other one pulls, creating a 
shearing-type force in the tissue planes. Rolling involves grabbing 
a small amount of tissue between the finger pads and thumb and 
rolling the tissue as if moving a small object under the skin 
(Fig. 17.19). Shaking is a technique in which the tissue is grabbed 
and vigorously shaken between the hands. The hands are then re-
positioned along the course of the muscle being treated.
Tapotement. Tapotement, or percussion massage, uses rhyth-
mic alternating contact of varying pressure between the hands 
and the body’s soft tissue.
91,179 Various techniques are used to 
produce this type of massage, including hacking, clapping, beat -
ing, pounding, and vibration. Hacking involves using the ulnar 
aspect of the hands to alternately strike the body tissues. These 
rapid strokes at 2 to 6 Hz are delivered in a sequential pattern 
along the entire region to be treated ( Fig. 17.20). Cupping in -
volves the use of a cupped palm, which is percussed against the 
chest wall. This technique is frequently used to loosen secretions 
in disease processes, such as cystic fibrosis. Beating involves using 
a clenched fist to repetitively pummel the tissue. This is a very 
aggressive type of tapotement and is not frequently used.  T apping 
uses the finger pads, typically of the index and middle fingers, to 
percuss. The finger pads strike the underlying tissue in rapid suc -
cession. This technique is frequently used over the sinuses to 
loosen secretions. There are a variety of practice patterns used to 
perform these techniques.
63
Friction Massage. Friction massage is a circular, longitudinal, 
or transverse pressure applied by the fingers, thumb, or hypo-
thenar region of the hand to small areas.
156,179 Cross-friction 
massage is perpendicular to the fibers and was used extensively 
• Fig. 17.19 Petrissage massage (rolling). In this form of petrissage, the 
skin or muscle is gathered up between the fingers and thumb and rolled 
continuously; new skin and muscle are gathered.
by Cyriax. 91 Very little motion occurs at the fingertips  
overlying the skin. The tissues are massaged from superficial to 
deep by increasing the pressure applied. The goal of friction 
massage is to break down adhesions in scar tissue, loosen liga -
ments, and disable trigger points. It is often uncomfortable and 
can result in some bruising. Despite this, it is an effective treat -
ment for tendonitis or tendinopathy,
62 subacromial bursitis or 
adhesive capsulitis, 217 iliotibial band syndrome, plantar fasci -
itis,376 headache, 207 and trigger points as described by T ravell 
(Fig. 17.21). 346
Other Western Techniques
Other techniques that integrate massage and movement into a 
rehabilitation program to enhance structure and function include 
Alexander and Feldenkrais techniques, manual lymphatic drain -
age (MLD), MFR, and Rolfing. Although these techniques are 
not used as much as the common Swedish techniques, they are 
gaining in popularity.
Alexander Technique. F .M. Alexander (1869–1955) was a 
Shakespearean actor who had recurrent neck and vocal problems. 
After much self-reflection and postural reeducation, he noted that 
his vocal problems could be corrected by postural reeducation. He 
developed a series of techniques for the treatment of chronic neck

and low back pain. 84 His approach centered on balance between 
head and neck movement “primary control” and a state of dy -
namic postures and breathing exercises. This technique teaches 
patients to engage their mind to understand beneficial patterns 
and overcome motion patterns, which are considered automatic. It 
is thought that these nonbeneficial patterns can be broken through 
conscious training.87,313 There are recent trials showing the Alexan-
der techniques in conjunction with exercise and other treatments 
have some long-term benefit in managing recurrent low back pain 
and are more cost effective than other treatments. 26,180,240,367 It is 
also being studied for balance problems in the elderly and persons 
with Parkinson disease.367
Feldenkrais Pattern. Moshe Feldenkrais (1880–1967) was a 
physicist and mechanical engineer who had a knee dysfunction 
and was told that he most likely would have to be a wheelchair 
user. Using his black belt judo techniques and physics background, 
he studied movement and the interrelationship between muscle 
contraction and  motion. Through self-rehabilitation of his knee 
problem, he developed several treatment techniques that could be 
performed during functional tasks. The goals of these techniques 
were to learn efficient and pain-free motion. Feldenkrais empha -
sized multiple repetitions of these therapeutic movements to lay 
down new neuromuscular patterns, and he considered the entire 
body even in the simplest of movements. 191,313 There are recent 
data to support its use in patients with chronic low back pain, 278 
neck/scapular pain,244 and osteoarthritis. 360 The Feldenkrais pat -
tern can be learned as a self-management technique or performed 
in a group setting,273 so it is cost effective and is easily incorporated 
into a home exercise regimen.
Manual Lymphatic Drainage. MLD was developed in Europe 
in the 1930s by Danish physiotherapists Estrid and Emil  Vodder 
as a technique to control postmastectomy lymphedema. MLD is 
a gentle and superficially  focused massage in which lymph is 
moved from areas of lymphatic vessel damage to watershed re -
gions.53,221 The first part of the treatment involves massage of the 
proximal region of the extremity to be treated. This is thought to 
dilate the watershed lymph vessels and allows them to accept fluid 
from distal areas. 21,71,221 After proximal areas have been gently 
massaged, a more rhythmic massage is performed from a distal to 
proximal part of the extremity. The typical session lasts 45 to 
60  minutes. Long-term maintenance is required for sustained 
edema control. It has been demonstrated that the limb volume 
can be reduced by as much as 25% to 63% after this treat -
ment.341,343 Complex lymphedema therapy9,239,343 includes MLD 
combined with other modalities, such as sequential pumping, 
low-tension wraps, skin care, compressive garments, 255 exer -
cise,225,345 and even surgery. 100 Recent reviews would suggest 
MLD is safe and may provide enhanced edema reduction on top 
of compression bandaging or garments, 124 and there is a much 
better response when used with exercise.274 MLD is popular in the 
United States for treatment after mastectomy or other surgeries 
that remove some lymph nodes. Many therapists have become 
trained in these techniques and assist patients with various can -
cers. It is still being researched, though, as to whether MLD and/
or exercise is preventative for lymphedema following breast cancer 
surgery.111,299,333
Myofascial Release. The term “myofascial release” was coined 
by Robert Ward in the 1960s 359 and further developed by John 
Barnes, a physical therapist. 22 MFR is founded on the premise 
that the body is encased in connective tissue (i.e., fascia). Fascia is 
the ground substance that interconnects all bones, muscles, 
nerves, and other internal organs and tissues. Injury or tension 
within one area of the fascia can result in pain and tenderness. 
Because of the interconnections, such injury can also result in 
pain and dysfunction at a distant point. Practitioners of MFR use 
gentle low load, long-duration stretching, and massage to release 
the fascial tension and can often work on areas that seem unre -
lated to the primary pain or injury. MFR is frequently used to 
treat chronic pain and restore normal ROM and function after a 
variety of musculoskeletal injuries. Review of the literature finds a 
variable pattern to the quality and effectiveness of studies using 
MFR.5,95,277,314,349 There is currently strong interest in utilizing a 
self-MFR program to enhance flexibility and decrease postexercise 
muscle soreness.25,129,136,158,206,228,253,287,314 These trials show vari -
able success or responses, but would indicate that some people can 
benefit from this as a self-management strategy. This desire for 
independent self-management has led to the development of 
roller massage, which can improve ROM64,65,178,246,335 and reduce 
symptoms112,232,280,293,321,371 with independent utilization.
Rolfing Structural Integration.  Ida Rolf (1896–1979), a 
chemist who had health problems, was inspired by such med -
ical philosophies as osteopathy, Alexander technique, and 
yoga. She viewed the body as a group of units and studied their 
relationship to each other. Rolf saw gravity as one of the primary 
causes of dysfunction. The primary tenet of her structural integra-
tion system was to help clients achieve proper vertical alignment 
and efficient movement.84 The typical regimen consists of a series 
of 10 sessions (60 to 90 min/each). Superficial massage is per -
formed initially, with progression to deeper friction massage. This 
is in an attempt to stretch fascia and allow muscles to relax and 
lengthen.41 The sessions build on one another, and additional 
treatments are frequently required to accommodate for the 
changes promoted. The deep friction massage traditionally per -
formed with Rolfing techniques can be painful and lead to bruis -
ing, but newer techniques are less painful and less invasive.
Eastern Forms of Massage
Acupressure. Acupressure has been defined as digital pressure 
performed in a circular motion to treat areas that are typically 
treated with acupuncture needles and for the same reasons. 
 Acupuncture was developed more than 3000 years ago in China. 
The basic philosophy behind this technique is to restore energy flow 
(or qi). Qi has two basic components that flow along 12 meridians: 
yin is associated with passivity, rest, and cold; yang is associated 
with activity, stimulation, and heat. A balance of these forces is 
thought to be associated with health and an imbalance with disease. 
The goal of acupressure therapy is to restore energy balance or ho-
meostasis. Deep pressure is applied to the acupuncture points in a 
circular manner without the use of lubrication. Acupressure can be 
used for the treatment of nausea and vomiting during preg -
nancy,153,358 postoperatively,351 and with chemotherapy 144,368; to 
decrease labor,327 cancer,170 or postoperative pain185,266,272; for the 
treatment of headaches and sleep problems67,110,177,237,355,369; and to 
decrease temporomandibular joint pain. Acupressure is a technique 
that can easily be converted to a self-management modality.68 It is 
being trialed with self-performed pressure on the ear, which is 
known as auricular acupressure,222,353,372,378 or by using bands that 
apply pressure at Neiguan point 6 on the volar wrist.144,351 Many of 
the beneficial responses may be linked back to a reduction in anxi-
ety levels, including an ability to increase aerobic performance in 
healthy young males.4
Anma. Anma is a practice of traditional Japanese massage, 
thought to be of Chinese origin, and developed from tuina 
techniques as early as AD 700. It was popularized by Sugiyama

Waichi (1610–1694), a blind acupuncturist, who practiced and 
taught the anma techniques to many other blind practitioners. 
This form of massage incorporates techniques such as pressing/
stroking, grasping/kneading, stretching, compressing, vibrating, 
and tapping applied to meridians and specific points on the 
body.114 It may also include deep palpation of the abdomen or 
forceful pressure with the knuckles to acupressure points. The 
intended goal of anma is to increase blood flow to tissues. Its 
popularity has waxed and waned during different times of Japa -
nese culture and history, but many of the principles of anma have 
been incorporated into shiatsu and Western massage. It is most 
often performed through a person’s clothes as opposed to directly 
on the skin. Currently, use is for improving physical complaints 
and quality of life in survivors of gynecologic cancers 113 and alle-
viating physical symptoms in patients with Parkinson disease.11,113
Reflexology. Ancient medical practitioners hypothesized that 
there is a homuncular representation of the body on the sole of 
the feet.363 This philosophy probably dates back to ancient Egypt, 
but the Chinese medical literature demonstrated a homunculus  
of the body on the ears, feet, and hands. Palpations of specific 
areas on the feet were thought to be tender if their corresponding 
body part was dysfunctional. Reflexology is the application of 
deep circular pressure applied to specific dysfunctional points  
on the soles of the feet. This is considered a separate discipline in 
the United States but is used by some Swedish massage practitio -
ners to treat areas of extreme tenderness. It has been used to treat 
hypertension, stress/anxiety, 1,98 pain, 7,8 fatigue, and digestive 
complaints,189,211,375 with many more recent trials completed in 
patients with neurologic disorders. 298,308 A previous review of 
management for females in labor did not show definitive bene -
fit,327 but additional trials have been completed. 6,194,234 Recent 
reviews of a variety of reflexology techniques for chronic medical 
problems have not shown conclusive medical responses.330
Shiatsu. Shiatsu (or “finger pressure”) is the Japanese type of 
body work based on acupuncture with principles from tuina and 
anma. Like anma, this technique was initially practiced by visually 
impaired clinicians in Japan. Pressure is applied in particular me -
ridians similar to acupuncture by the thumbs, fingers, or palms, 
but it can be performed by leaning the body’s weight into various 
points and meridians. Modern shiatsu was promoted in the 1940s 
by T okujiro Namikoshi, and this type of treatment has been west-
ernized and is being increasingly used in the United States. There 
is only limited scientific evidence of its effectiveness in very spe -
cific patients.41,167,231,294,312
Thai Massage. Thai massage is believed to have been devel -
oped by Shivago Komarpaj over 2500 years ago and included 
his work on Buddha himself. Currently, Thai massage is taught 
in Thailand at the Wat Pro Thai T raditional Medical and Massage 
School and incorporated into mainstream medical management 
there, as well as now practiced in many other countries. It is per -
formed on patients who remain clothed and does not include oils 
or lotions. The practitioner compresses, pulls, stretches, or rocks 
the body rather than rubbing. 204 There is increasing evidence for 
its use in musculoskeletal disorders, 13,46,47,334 pain, 59,214,286 and 
other medical problems.60,210,332
Tuina. T uina is an ancient Chinese manipulative therapy 
used in an attempt to open the body’s defensive chi (weiqi) to 
allow energy to move along the meridians and in the muscles. 
Tui means “to push,” and na means “to lift and squeeze.” Prac -
titioners apply techniques such as rubbing, pressing, rolling, 
kneading, brushing, shaking, and tapotement between joints  
to impact acute and chronic musculoskeletal problems. T uina 
often is  combined with traditional Chinese treatments, includ -
ing acupuncture, tai chi, qigong, herbalism, and cupping, or 
with Western techniques, such as traction, ROM, and other 
manual treatments. It is an ancient form of treatment that is 
continuing to be used for headaches, constipation, irritable 
bowel syndrome, insomnia/sleep  problems, 127,243 psychological 
disorders, vascular compliance, 210 and negative effects of neuro -
logic disorders. 66,143,236,357,374
Evidence-Based Use of Massage
Massage has been used in multiple forms for the treatment of many 
conditions. T raditional research is difficult to perform on these tech-
niques because of the lack of a standardized sham treatment. Al -
though there is a paucity of quality research on these techniques, 
there has been a renewed interest in establishing the efficacy of mas-
sage with many published trials. At least one large randomized con-
trolled trial supports the use of massage in the treatment of anxiety 
and stress,67,78,97,237,294,369 elevated blood pressure,149,317,318 arthralgias 
and various arthritides,15,130,131,132,268,284,285,286,292 acute postoperative 
pain,1,17,36,37 fibromyalgia,99,148,349 lymphedema,125,239,274,341 balance 
problems,318 sleep disorders,43,127 musculoskeletal disorders such as 
neck and low back pain,13,47,70,95,124,141,190,195 and sports-related inju-
ries.123 There has also been extensive research into the use of massage 
for recovery after strenuous exercise,129,159 which shows some effec-
tiveness, but its use to enhance physical performance is still with 
limited validation. 4,123,136,253,264,324 There are recommendations  
suggesting that massage therapy might be useful as an adjunct treat-
ment or possible alternative treatment for the following conditions 
(research data for these are less compelling than a randomized 
 controlled trial): burn care, 16,23,97,133,173 care of people with 
 cancer,68,113,170,227,239,271,341,343,364,368 chronic pain,28,41,43,95,98,141,205,214 
exercise-induced injury,301,328 headaches,59,145,207,344 other neurologic 
disorders,11,66,142,236,267,298,308,357,374 and HIV infection 143 and 
AIDS.34,309 Although massage therapy is obviously not curative in 
these conditions, it may enhance the effectiveness of other therapeu-
tic interventions. There is currently strong interest in combining 
massage with other treatments such as aromatherapy in persons with 
cancer.48,323 Massage combined with aromatherapy has been shown 
to be beneficial in many musculoskeletal conditions,117,152 but when 
combined with exercise it can often lead to a more long-term benefit 
for patients.13,46,200,276,286,292
Contraindications to Massage
Massage therapy is a relatively safe modality. Complications are 
rare and usually not serious. There are several absolute and relative 
contraindications to traditional Swedish massage. Potential com -
plications for Eastern forms of massage, such as acupressure, 
anma, tuina, and Thai massage, and combined treatments, such as 
Alexander and Feldenkrais, have not been specifically delineated. 
Massage should not be performed over areas of malignancy, cel -
lulitis, or lymphangitis.220 The effect of massage on these regions 
can cause mobilization of tumor cells into the vascular lymphatic 
supply or the spread of infection.
Areas of trauma or recent bleeding should not be treated with 
deep tissue massage. Mobilization of these areas can increase the 
propensity for rebleeding. Patients who are taking anticoagulants 
should be treated with gentler techniques and observed for bruis -
ing and ecchymosis. Deep tissue work should be used with ex -
treme caution in those receiving anticoagulants or who have a 
bleeding diathesis. If neurologic changes occur after any massage

canal or around a peripheral nerve.
Massage should not be used over areas of known deep venous 
thrombosis or atherosclerotic plaques. This could result in dis -
lodgment of these vascular thrombi, resulting in embolic infarcts 
affecting the pulmonary, cerebral, or peripheral systems. Special 
care should be observed in patients with osteoarthritis or severe 
osteoporosis to avoid any excess ROM or stretching that could 
alter articulating surfaces. Patients with low blood pressure might 
experience postural hypotension after treatment and should be 
observed carefully. Patients who have been physically or sexually 
abused can reexperience elements of trauma during treatment. 
Special care regarding touch is especially warranted with these 
individuals. People with edema should not undergo deep tissue 
massage or any other massage technique that could result in local 
accumulation of interstitial fluid.
Summary
Manipulation, traction, and massage have been an integral part of 
health care since ancient times. The popularity of these techniques 
ebb and flow with changes in the traditional medical paradigm of 
the time. They have enjoyed a resurgence in popularity in recent 
years with clinician awareness of the need to address pain non -
pharmacologically and associated problems such as sleep and 
mood disorders. Research efforts, although still developing, have 
shown that a spectrum of beneficial physiologic and clinical 
changes can be associated with these modalities. Manipulation, 
traction, and massage are becoming increasingly recognized as a 
valuable adjunct to standard medical care for specific musculo -
skeletal problems and may help to reduce the use of pharmaco -
logic treatments for these problems or the side effects of many 
treatments for more complex medical problems.
